ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper applies established probabilistic modelling (Gaussian Processes, Warped GPs, Matèrn kernels) and standard Bayesian decision theory to the QE task, so it mainly adapts known methods to a new application rather than proposing a fundamentally new method. Framing NLPD as a useful metric for QE and demonstrating Bayes estimators under asymmetric losses offers a useful perspective, but this is an incremental methodological contribution rather than a breakthrough. The experiments and claims appear to explore practical modelling choices for small QE datasets, which would inform experts but is not surprising to those familiar with probabilistic regression and uncertainty-aware decision-making. | B(3): The paper brings a moderate novelty by applying probabilistic Gaussian Process models (including Warped GPs and Matérn52 kernels) to Machine Translation Quality Estimation and by promoting NLPD as an evaluation that leverages full predictive distributions. However, GPs, warped GPs, and the use of predictive uncertainty for decision-making under asymmetric loss are established techniques; the work mainly adapts these known methods to QE rather than proposing fundamentally new modelling or inference methods. The clear framing of asymmetric loss scenarios and empirical focus on calibration are useful to practitioners, but the contribution is best characterized as an incremental, application-driven advance rather than a significant methodological breakthrough.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper applies established probabilistic modelling (Gaussian Processes, Warped GPs, Matèrn kernels) and standard Bayesian decision theory to the QE task, so it mainly adapts known methods to a new application rather than proposing a fundamentally new method. Framing NLPD as a useful metric for QE and demonstrating Bayes estimators under asymmetric losses offers a useful perspective, but this is an incremental methodological contribution rather than a breakthrough. The experiments and claims appear to explore practical modelling choices for small QE datasets, which would inform experts but is not surprising to those familiar with probabilistic regression and uncertainty-aware decision-making.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper brings a moderate novelty by applying probabilistic Gaussian Process models (including Warped GPs and Matérn52 kernels) to Machine Translation Quality Estimation and by promoting NLPD as an evaluation that leverages full predictive distributions. However, GPs, warped GPs, and the use of predictive uncertainty for decision-making under asymmetric loss are established techniques; the work mainly adapts these known methods to QE rather than proposing fundamentally new modelling or inference methods. The clear framing of asymmetric loss scenarios and empirical focus on calibration are useful to practitioners, but the contribution is best characterized as an incremental, application-driven advance rather than a significant methodological breakthrough.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper addresses a clearly relevant NLP/ML topic—probabilistic uncertainty estimation for machine translation quality estimation—and uses methods (Gaussian Processes, Warped GPs, NLPD/NLL, asymmetric loss analysis) and metrics that the conference audience expects. The experimental setup (standard QuEst features, ARD hyperparameter tuning, 10-fold CV) is appropriate, though small QE datasets (≈2.5k instances) and no mention of code release limit reproducibility and practical impact. Overall it is a good fit for a top venue with minor concerns about dataset scale and reproducibility details. | B(4): This paper sits squarely within the NLP/ML conference remit: it addresses machine translation quality estimation with probabilistic modelling (Gaussian and Warped GPs), introduces evaluation with NLPD, and explores decision-making under asymmetric losses—topics of clear interest to both ML and NLP researchers. The methods, metrics (NLPD, NLL, MAE, Pearson r), and cross-validated evaluation match venue expectations, and the framing toward pragmatic translation workflows is appropriate for the community. My only minor concern is the limited dataset scale (single small QE datasets reported) and incremental novelty relative to existing probabilistic QE work, which makes it a good but not perfect fit for a top-tier venue.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses a clearly relevant NLP/ML topic—probabilistic uncertainty estimation for machine translation quality estimation—and uses methods (Gaussian Processes, Warped GPs, NLPD/NLL, asymmetric loss analysis) and metrics that the conference audience expects. The experimental setup (standard QuEst features, ARD hyperparameter tuning, 10-fold CV) is appropriate, though small QE datasets (≈2.5k instances) and no mention of code release limit reproducibility and practical impact. Overall it is a good fit for a top venue with minor concerns about dataset scale and reproducibility details.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper sits squarely within the NLP/ML conference remit: it addresses machine translation quality estimation with probabilistic modelling (Gaussian and Warped GPs), introduces evaluation with NLPD, and explores decision-making under asymmetric losses—topics of clear interest to both ML and NLP researchers. The methods, metrics (NLPD, NLL, MAE, Pearson r), and cross-validated evaluation match venue expectations, and the framing toward pragmatic translation workflows is appropriate for the community. My only minor concern is the limited dataset scale (single small QE datasets reported) and incremental novelty relative to existing probabilistic QE work, which makes it a good but not perfect fit for a top-tier venue.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well-organized and the main contributions are stated early (probabilistic GPs for QE, use of NLPD, and asymmetric loss analysis), which makes the narrative easy to follow. Notation and methodology (GPs, Warped GPs, ARD hyperparameter tuning with 10 restarts, 10-fold CV) are clearly described and hyperparameters are reported, supporting reproducibility. A few presentation choices—such as omitting some kernel/weighting results for brevity—reduce completeness but do not materially hinder understanding; overall the writing is clear and grammatically good. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and reads clearly with a logical flow from introduction through methods, experiments and conclusions; main contributions are stated early and related work is adequate. Notation and methodological details (e.g. GP variants, hyperparameter optimisation and ARD tuning) are reported, and figures/tables appear effective though some experimental choices (e.g. omitting kernel/weighting variants and not retraining for asymmetric-loss experiments) are justified but could use clearer signposting. A few presentation omissions—such as no mention of code release and selective reporting of results—limit full reproducibility and leave some sections feeling under-detailed for a practitioner audience. Overall writing quality is good with minor clarity issues in the experimental reporting.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well-organized and the main contributions are stated early (probabilistic GPs for QE, use of NLPD, and asymmetric loss analysis), which makes the narrative easy to follow. Notation and methodology (GPs, Warped GPs, ARD hyperparameter tuning with 10 restarts, 10-fold CV) are clearly described and hyperparameters are reported, supporting reproducibility. A few presentation choices—such as omitting some kernel/weighting results for brevity—reduce completeness but do not materially hinder understanding; overall the writing is clear and grammatically good.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and reads clearly with a logical flow from introduction through methods, experiments and conclusions; main contributions are stated early and related work is adequate. Notation and methodological details (e.g. GP variants, hyperparameter optimisation and ARD tuning) are reported, and figures/tables appear effective though some experimental choices (e.g. omitting kernel/weighting variants and not retraining for asymmetric-loss experiments) are justified but could use clearer signposting. A few presentation omissions—such as no mention of code release and selective reporting of results—limit full reproducibility and leave some sections feeling under-detailed for a practitioner audience. Overall writing quality is good with minor clarity issues in the experimental reporting.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against standard and relevant baselines for QE (SVR and Bagging) and evaluates under the same data splits and metrics (MAE, Pearson r, NLPD/NLL), with careful cross-validation and hyperparameter tuning, so comparisons are generally fair. However, it omits more recent or stronger QE baselines (e.g., neural or other contemporary probabilistic approaches) that would be expected given the claim focus on uncertainty. The authors acknowledge when methods produce similar trends (kernel/weighting results omitted) and analyze differences via distributional metrics and Bayes estimators, but the absence of newer competing models is a notable omission. | B(4): The paper compares probabilistic GPs against standard and sensible baselines for QE (SVR and bagging) on the same datasets and evaluation metrics (MAE, Pearson r, NLPD) and reports hyperparameter/experimental settings, so comparisons are largely fair. However, it omits more recent neural and end-to-end QE approaches that have become relevant even for small-data regimes, which is a notable omission given the field’s movement; the authors do acknowledge when different kernel/weighting choices produce similar trends and report limitations of dataset size. Overall coverage of related work is described as adequate, and the paper analyzes differences in predictive distributions and decision-making under asymmetric losses, but the absence of stronger contemporary baselines prevents a fully comprehensive evaluation.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against standard and relevant baselines for QE (SVR and Bagging) and evaluates under the same data splits and metrics (MAE, Pearson r, NLPD/NLL), with careful cross-validation and hyperparameter tuning, so comparisons are generally fair. However, it omits more recent or stronger QE baselines (e.g., neural or other contemporary probabilistic approaches) that would be expected given the claim focus on uncertainty. The authors acknowledge when methods produce similar trends (kernel/weighting results omitted) and analyze differences via distributional metrics and Bayes estimators, but the absence of newer competing models is a notable omission.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares probabilistic GPs against standard and sensible baselines for QE (SVR and bagging) on the same datasets and evaluation metrics (MAE, Pearson r, NLPD) and reports hyperparameter/experimental settings, so comparisons are largely fair. However, it omits more recent neural and end-to-end QE approaches that have become relevant even for small-data regimes, which is a notable omission given the field’s movement; the authors do acknowledge when different kernel/weighting choices produce similar trends and report limitations of dataset size. Overall coverage of related work is described as adequate, and the paper analyzes differences in predictive distributions and decision-making under asymmetric losses, but the absence of stronger contemporary baselines prevents a fully comprehensive evaluation.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper conducts a focused empirical study (Gaussian Processes, Warped GPs, Matèrn52 kernel, 10 random restarts, ARD fine-tuning, 10-fold CV) and uses appropriate probabilistic metrics (NLPD/NLL) and realistic asymmetric decision analyses. However, important reproducibility and sensitivity elements are missing or incomplete: code is not provided, some kernel/weighting results were omitted, asymmetric-loss experiments used no retraining, and dataset scope is limited (one small fr-en set explicitly reported ~2.5k examples). Given these gaps, the experiments are adequate to support the core claims qualitatively but lack the depth, ablations, and reproducibility required for a thoroughly convincing evaluation. | B(4): The paper presents solid empirical evidence that probabilistic GPs yield calibrated uncertainty for QE and introduces NLPD and asymmetric-loss Bayes decisions, evaluated against reasonable baselines (SVR, bagging) on standard QE data (fr-en; en-de mentioned) with full hyperparameter optimisation. It lacks some important sensitivity/ablation detail (e.g., kernel and warping ablation results were omitted, asymmetric-loss experiments used post-hoc predictions without retraining), and code is not reported which hinders exact reproducibility despite hyperparameter disclosure. Related work and limitations are discussed, so overall the experimental work is strong and practically oriented but missing a few analyses and reproducibility artifacts that would make it fully comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper conducts a focused empirical study (Gaussian Processes, Warped GPs, Matèrn52 kernel, 10 random restarts, ARD fine-tuning, 10-fold CV) and uses appropriate probabilistic metrics (NLPD/NLL) and realistic asymmetric decision analyses. However, important reproducibility and sensitivity elements are missing or incomplete: code is not provided, some kernel/weighting results were omitted, asymmetric-loss experiments used no retraining, and dataset scope is limited (one small fr-en set explicitly reported ~2.5k examples). Given these gaps, the experiments are adequate to support the core claims qualitatively but lack the depth, ablations, and reproducibility required for a thoroughly convincing evaluation.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents solid empirical evidence that probabilistic GPs yield calibrated uncertainty for QE and introduces NLPD and asymmetric-loss Bayes decisions, evaluated against reasonable baselines (SVR, bagging) on standard QE data (fr-en; en-de mentioned) with full hyperparameter optimisation. It lacks some important sensitivity/ablation detail (e.g., kernel and warping ablation results were omitted, asymmetric-loss experiments used post-hoc predictions without retraining), and code is not reported which hinders exact reproducibility despite hyperparameter disclosure. Related work and limitations are discussed, so overall the experimental work is strong and practically oriented but missing a few analyses and reproducibility artifacts that would make it fully comprehensive.

REPLICABILITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper reports hyperparameter optimisation (marginal likelihood with 10 random restarts and ARD fine‑tuning) and states model choices (Warped GPs, Matèrn52) and evaluation procedures (NLPD, NLL, MAE, Pearson, 10‑fold CV, Bayes estimators on CV predictions), so core modelling and evaluation details are provided. However, the preprocessing pipeline is not clearly described (only ‘standard QE features (QuEst)’ is mentioned), code is not provided, and one dataset (en–de) is only mentioned without full specification—risking replication gaps such as exact feature extraction, random seeds, and training/run scripts. Given these mostly-complete but some important omissions, the work is largely reproducible but would benefit from released code and explicit preprocessing and dataset details. | B(4): Overall the paper provides many key reproducibility elements: hyperparameter optimisation procedure (marginal likelihood, 10 random restarts, ARD fine‑tuning) and GP choices (Warped GPs, Matèrn52) are reported, datasets (Specia fr‑en QE) and standard QuEst features are specified, and evaluation metrics (NLPD, NLL, MAE, Pearson r) and 10‑fold CV protocol are given. However, important practical details are under‑specified or omitted in the summary: the exact feature preprocessing pipeline and any feature selection/normalisation steps are not described, code is not released, and some modelling choices/results (other kernels/weighting schemes) were omitted which may affect reimplementation. Given these minor but meaningful gaps, the work is mostly reproducible but would benefit from code and fuller data/preprocessing details.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper reports hyperparameter optimisation (marginal likelihood with 10 random restarts and ARD fine‑tuning) and states model choices (Warped GPs, Matèrn52) and evaluation procedures (NLPD, NLL, MAE, Pearson, 10‑fold CV, Bayes estimators on CV predictions), so core modelling and evaluation details are provided. However, the preprocessing pipeline is not clearly described (only ‘standard QE features (QuEst)’ is mentioned), code is not provided, and one dataset (en–de) is only mentioned without full specification—risking replication gaps such as exact feature extraction, random seeds, and training/run scripts. Given these mostly-complete but some important omissions, the work is largely reproducible but would benefit from released code and explicit preprocessing and dataset details.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper provides many key reproducibility elements: hyperparameter optimisation procedure (marginal likelihood, 10 random restarts, ARD fine‑tuning) and GP choices (Warped GPs, Matèrn52) are reported, datasets (Specia fr‑en QE) and standard QuEst features are specified, and evaluation metrics (NLPD, NLL, MAE, Pearson r) and 10‑fold CV protocol are given. However, important practical details are under‑specified or omitted in the summary: the exact feature preprocessing pipeline and any feature selection/normalisation steps are not described, code is not released, and some modelling choices/results (other kernels/weighting schemes) were omitted which may affect reimplementation. Given these minor but meaningful gaps, the work is mostly reproducible but would benefit from code and fuller data/preprocessing details.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): This paper addresses a practically important problem—quantifying uncertainty in machine translation quality estimation—and demonstrates that probabilistic models (Gaussian Processes, including Warped GPs and Matèrn52 kernels) can yield well-calibrated predictive distributions and support decision-making under asymmetric losses. Introducing NLPD as an evaluation metric and showing how Bayes estimators can be applied to cross-validated predictive distributions are useful contributions that can influence how QE systems are evaluated and deployed. However, the empirical setting is limited (small QE datasets, no retraining for asymmetric-loss experiments, and only selective kernel reporting), which constrains generality and immediate adoption; thus the impact is moderate rather than transformative. | B(3): The paper addresses an important practical problem—providing calibrated uncertainty in MT quality estimation—and demonstrates useful tools (GPs, NLPD) and decision-aware uses (asymmetric losses) that could change how practitioners treat QE outputs in workflows. By emphasizing full predictive distributions and decision rules, it offers actionable guidance likely to spur follow-up work and applied adoption. However, impact is moderated by experiments on small, limited datasets, a narrow set of baselines and the lack of retraining experiments or broader multilingual validation, so its influence is more evolutionary than transformative.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses a practically important problem—quantifying uncertainty in machine translation quality estimation—and demonstrates that probabilistic models (Gaussian Processes, including Warped GPs and Matèrn52 kernels) can yield well-calibrated predictive distributions and support decision-making under asymmetric losses. Introducing NLPD as an evaluation metric and showing how Bayes estimators can be applied to cross-validated predictive distributions are useful contributions that can influence how QE systems are evaluated and deployed. However, the empirical setting is limited (small QE datasets, no retraining for asymmetric-loss experiments, and only selective kernel reporting), which constrains generality and immediate adoption; thus the impact is moderate rather than transformative.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses an important practical problem—providing calibrated uncertainty in MT quality estimation—and demonstrates useful tools (GPs, NLPD) and decision-aware uses (asymmetric losses) that could change how practitioners treat QE outputs in workflows. By emphasizing full predictive distributions and decision rules, it offers actionable guidance likely to spur follow-up work and applied adoption. However, impact is moderated by experiments on small, limited datasets, a narrow set of baselines and the lack of retraining experiments or broader multilingual validation, so its influence is more evolutionary than transformative.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by empirical evidence: probabilistic GPs (including warped GPs) were evaluated with appropriate probabilistic metrics (NLPD/NLL) and compared against sensible baselines (SVR, bagging) using 10-fold CV and hyperparameter restarts. However, from the summary I see some methodological gaps that prevent a flawless verdict: no mention of statistical significance testing or variance reporting across restarts/folds, omitted kernel/weighting results (potential selective reporting), and the asymmetric-loss experiments relied on applying Bayes estimators to fixed CV predictions without retraining which could bias conclusions about deployment. Mathematical treatment appears standard and correct, but reproducibility is weakened by lack of code/seeds and incomplete result reporting. These are important but do not fatally undermine the main empirical claim, hence a score of 4. | B(4): The paper's central claims—that probabilistic GPs yield useful calibrated uncertainties for QE and that NLPD captures distributional quality beyond point estimates—are supported by experimental comparisons (GP variants vs SVR and Bagging) on standard QE features and datasets (fr-en, en-de) and by reporting NLPD/NLL alongside MAE and Pearson. Mathematical treatment appears standard for GPs and optimisation via marginal likelihood with ARD is appropriate; I see no indication of incorrect derivations. Methodologically there are minor shortcomings: small QE datasets, omission of statistical significance testing, and the asymmetric-loss analysis used post-hoc Bayes estimators without retraining, which weakens claims about decision-making in realistic workflows. Overall the results justify the conclusions but these limitations should be addressed to fully validate practical impact.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by empirical evidence: probabilistic GPs (including warped GPs) were evaluated with appropriate probabilistic metrics (NLPD/NLL) and compared against sensible baselines (SVR, bagging) using 10-fold CV and hyperparameter restarts. However, from the summary I see some methodological gaps that prevent a flawless verdict: no mention of statistical significance testing or variance reporting across restarts/folds, omitted kernel/weighting results (potential selective reporting), and the asymmetric-loss experiments relied on applying Bayes estimators to fixed CV predictions without retraining which could bias conclusions about deployment. Mathematical treatment appears standard and correct, but reproducibility is weakened by lack of code/seeds and incomplete result reporting. These are important but do not fatally undermine the main empirical claim, hence a score of 4.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's central claims—that probabilistic GPs yield useful calibrated uncertainties for QE and that NLPD captures distributional quality beyond point estimates—are supported by experimental comparisons (GP variants vs SVR and Bagging) on standard QE features and datasets (fr-en, en-de) and by reporting NLPD/NLL alongside MAE and Pearson. Mathematical treatment appears standard for GPs and optimisation via marginal likelihood with ARD is appropriate; I see no indication of incorrect derivations. Methodologically there are minor shortcomings: small QE datasets, omission of statistical significance testing, and the asymmetric-loss analysis used post-hoc Bayes estimators without retraining, which weakens claims about decision-making in realistic workflows. Overall the results justify the conclusions but these limitations should be addressed to fully validate practical impact.

main_claims: Probabilistic models (Gaussian Processes) can provide well-calibrated uncertainty estimates for Machine Translation Quality Estimation (QE).

Negative Log Predictive Density (NLPD) is a useful evaluation metric for QE as it captures information from the full predictive distribution and can distinguish models with similar point-estimate performance.

Predictive uncertainty from probabilistic QE models can be employed together with asymmetric loss functions (e.g., asymmetric linear and linex losses) to better address realistic pessimistic and optimistic decision-making scenarios in translation workflows.
Warped Gaussian Processes and different kernel choices (e.g., Matèrn52) are practical modelling choices explored to improve probabilistic QE.
_summary.methodology: Apply Gaussian Process regression (including Warped GPs) to Machine Translation Quality Estimation using standard QE features (QuEst), optimise hyperparameters via marginal likelihood (with 10 random restarts and ARD fine-tuning), evaluate predictive distributions using NLPD and NLL, and assess decision-making under asymmetric losses via Bayes estimators on 10-fold cross-validated predictions.
limitations: QE datasets are usually small (on the order of thousands of instances), which affects modelling choices.
For asymmetric loss experiments no retraining was performed; Bayes estimators were applied to collected cross-validated predictions.
Findings for different kernels and weighting schemes showed similar trends and were omitted for clarity (i.e., results reported only for Matèrn52).

reviewer_a.name: Statistical ML Engineer
reviewer_a.background: PhD-level ML engineer with 8+ years building and evaluating probabilistic models for NLP and industrial ML systems; experience shipping calibrated uncertainty estimators in production.
traits: insists on rigorous experimental design: clear train/validation/test splits, cross-validation details, and avoidance of data leakage
checks statistical soundness: proper baselines, significance/variance reporting, and ablation studies for modelling choices
values reproducibility: requests code/seed/hyperparameter disclosure and deterministic training protocols
focuses on calibration and evaluation methodology: careful use and interpretation of probabilistic metrics (NLPD, NLL, calibration curves) and robustness to hyperparameter restarts
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: I evaluate papers by probing experimental rigor, reproducibility, and whether the empirical evidence truly supports the statistical claims.

reviewer_b.name: Applied MT & QE Researcher
reviewer_b.background: Senior researcher in machine translation and quality estimation with 12+ years of experience bridging academic research and translation industry needs; has published on QE benchmarks and deployment use-cases.
traits: assesses novelty and positioning: compares proposed ideas against prior QE and probabilistic modelling work and expects clear articulation of contributions
values practical impact: examines whether the proposed uncertainty estimates address real-world decision problems and realistic cost models
examines metric relevance and user scenarios: scrutinizes whether evaluation tasks and asymmetric losses match deployment priorities and user workflows
looks for clarity in limitations and scalability: notices computational cost, data requirements, and how methods generalize across languages/domains
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: I judge papers by their novelty relative to prior work and by how convincingly they translate technical advances into practical benefits for MT and QE practitioners.
